summary: Cross-chapter audit of cycle 03 manuscript (17 chapters, 10,261 lines, ~102,891 prose words). REDUNDANCY: 28 findings across character re-introduction (5), setting re-description (3), proof-of-baseline accumulation (4), image/motif density (4), character gesture repetition (4), and hedge-word density (8). Key patterns: Loid's 'reading glasses folded' appears in near-identical construction across Ch1/Ch6/Ch17; reception-venue arrivals recycle the same warm-light/crystal/quartet/white-linens template across 5 venues; 'something' appears 141 times (1 per 730 words, exceeding 1/800 threshold); Damian's jaw-tightening appears in 4 chapters. CONSISTENCY: 4 findings. One HIGH: Yor kills four men in Ch15 but Ch17 says 'three men.' Three MEDIUM: Damian's memorabilia box inventoried three times with different contents (Ch7/Ch13/Ch16); class photo row position contradicts between Ch7 and Ch16. Em-dash density (0.6%) and sentence-opener monotony ('She' at 5.6%) are both well below thresholds. No near-verbatim cross-chapter passages of 10+ words found. Spatial and geographic continuity is flawless across all 17 chapters.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch1 L161: 'Reading glasses on the chain around her neck'; Ch6 L3810: 'reading glasses on the chain, coffee long cold, fluorescent light flattening everything in sight'
redundancy_findings.problem: Sylvia's 'reading glasses on the chain' is established as a distinguishing physical detail in Ch1 L161. Ch6 L3810 re-states the exact same phrase in Anya's mental image during a phone call. The reader already carries this detail from Ch1.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut or replace the glasses-on-chain detail at L3810. Anya's mental image of Sylvia can invoke posture, voice quality, or the desk itself rather than repeating the Ch1 physical tag.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: The phrase 'reading glasses on the chain' or synonymous phrasing does not appear in Ch6.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch1 L223: 'Reading glasses folded beside his cup'; Ch6 L3610: 'Loid's reading glasses sat folded on a stack of newspapers'
redundancy_findings.problem: Loid's 'reading glasses folded' is introduced as a specific character tag in Ch1 L223. Ch6 L3610 repeats the same prop in nearly identical construction ('reading glasses sat folded'). This is the first of three manuscript repetitions of this construction.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In the apartment inventory at L3610, fold the reading-glasses detail into the annotation system description without re-naming them as 'reading glasses,' or replace with a different Loid-specific domestic prop.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch6 does not re-state 'reading glasses folded' or synonymous phrasing of the Ch1 detail.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch1 L223: 'Reading glasses folded beside his cup'; Ch6 L3610: 'reading glasses sat folded on a stack of newspapers'; Ch17 L9849: 'Loid's reading glasses folded on top of a dish towel'; Ch17 L9933: 'Loid rubbed a thumb over the folded arm of his glasses'
redundancy_findings.problem: Third and fourth manuscript instances of Loid's 'reading glasses folded' as character tag. Ch17 uses the same construction twice within one chapter (L9849 kitchen inventory, L9933 confrontation). The construction 'reading glasses folded' has become a noticeable prose tic across Ch1/Ch6/Ch17.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Keep the confrontation scene glasses usage (L9911 or L9933) where they serve as emotional prop. Cut the kitchen-inventory instance at L9849. Ensure the surviving instance does not replicate the 'folded' construction from Ch1 and Ch6.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch17 contains at most one reading-glasses reference, and it avoids the 'reading glasses folded' phrasing.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch12 L6264: 'The thin scar through his eyebrow was the only thing that didn't match the packaging'; Ch13 L6785: 'The fluorescent light made the scar over his eyebrow look whiter than the rest of him'
redundancy_findings.problem: Damian's eyebrow scar is introduced as a permanent distinguishing feature in Ch12 L6264 and re-described one chapter later in Ch13 L6785. Adjacent-chapter repetition of the same distinguishing physical feature is the most noticeable form of re-description.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: At Ch13 L6785, cut or redirect the fluorescent-light detail to a different physical element (hands, posture, fatigue) that does not re-introduce the scar the reader just learned about in Ch12.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch13 does not re-describe the eyebrow scar as a visual distinguishing feature.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch1 L17: 'Anya couldn't turn it off. Hadn't been able to since she was four'; Ch8 L4332: 'The startle of other people's thoughts had burned off years ago. What stayed was the drag of it, the low constant pull, like carrying a radio under her ribs that never shut up for long'; Ch8 L4435: 'headache behind her right eye had already started its steady drilling'; Ch2 L394: 'Pressure gathered behind her left eye almost at once'
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch8 opens with two telepathy-burden re-statements. L4332 re-explains that telepathy is constant and unwanted (established Ch1 L17). L4435 deploys the headache-at-reception template for the third time (after Ch1 L111 and Ch2 L394). Both deliver information the reader already carries. The radio-under-ribs metaphor at L4332 is fresh but the payload is redundant.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut the explanatory re-statement at L4332. Trust the reader to carry the Ch1 establishment. At L4435, enter the reception through the specific crowd-noise content rather than re-proving that crowds give Anya headaches. Vary or cut the headache onset if the Ch2 version (pressure behind eye) already covers it.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch8's opening does not re-explain that telepathy is constant/unwanted and does not deploy the headache-behind-the-eye template a third time.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch2 L392: 'Warm gold light sat on the floor. White linens glowed under crystal. A quartet near the windows played a Baroque piece nobody cared about'; Ch8 L4407: 'Warm amber light hit first, then crystal, then bodies... The quartet was playing hard for people who did not care'
redundancy_findings.problem: The Concordia reception at Ch8 L4407 is the fourth reception-venue arrival in eight chapters and closely parallels Ch2's Ministry ballroom: warm-toned light + crystal + quartet + crowd indifference. The descriptive template (warm light, crystal, quartet nobody appreciates) has been used at Ch2 (Ministry), Ch3 (Academy), Ch5 (Gesellschaft Club), and now Ch8.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Enter the Concordia through what is operationally different: the exits, the SSS positioning, the target configuration. Cut or compress the warm-light/crystal/quartet inventory. The reader already knows diplomatic receptions have these elements.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch8's venue arrival does not re-inventory warm-toned light, crystal, and quartet as a descriptive set.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch2 L392: 'chandeliers... White linens glowed under crystal'; Ch12 L6336: 'a chandelier heavy enough to go on a threat map... Tables with white cloths ran along one wall. The buffet ran along the other. The bar already had the thickest crowd'
redundancy_findings.problem: The Blackbell estate ballroom at Ch12 L6336 is the fifth reception venue. Chandelier (third explicit description), white table coverings (second after Ch2), and bar-as-crowd-magnet (second after Ch8) are recycled venue elements. The underlying inventory repeats even when individual phrases are fresh.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Enter the Blackbell ballroom through Becky's personality stamped on the space, the Eden-reunion atmosphere, or the social tension. Cut the chandelier/white-cloths/bar inventory.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch12's venue arrival does not re-inventory chandelier, white table coverings, and bar crowd.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch1 L341: 'Outside, Berlint was going amber. Early autumn evening... Leaves on the cobblestones'; Ch2 L370: 'Berlint at dusk looked almost forgiving. Streetlights were coming up under a flat grey sky. Leaves scraped along the pavement in thin circles'
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch1 ending and Ch2 opening both describe Berlint at dusk in autumn with leaves on the ground. These are consecutive chapters — the reader encounters the same cityscape motif twice in immediate succession. Both passages include evening light quality, autumn atmosphere, and leaves on pavement.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Thin the Ch1 ending cityscape (L341) to one transitional line, since Ch2 opens with the same motif within 20 lines of reading. Alternatively, cut the Berlint-at-dusk framing from Ch2 L370 and enter the Ministry scene directly from the car.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: The Ch1-to-Ch2 transition does not present two full Berlint-at-dusk-with-leaves atmospheric descriptions in immediate succession.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch12 L6127: 'Damian's voice came on stripped of its diplomatic polish'; Ch12 L6456: 'Smile at the wall. Say we all look young' (performing at party); Ch12 L6528: 'His face stayed perfectly diplomatic. The thought under it did not'
redundancy_findings.problem: Damian's diplomatic-mask/polish baseline is proved three times within Ch12 alone (L6127, L6456, L6528). The mask-vs-reality contrast has been independently proved in 7 chapters (Ch2, Ch4, Ch5, Ch7, Ch8, Ch9, Ch12). Ch12's local cluster of three instances in one chapter is the densest accumulation.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Keep one diplomatic-mask reference in Ch12 (L6528 is strongest because it pairs face and thought). Cut or vary L6127 and L6456 to avoid re-proving the reader already knows Damian performs in public.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch12 contains at most one diplomatic-mask/polish reference rather than three.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch1 L335: 'Yor's culinary experiments were acts of love that frequently crossed the line into acts of violence against ingredients'; Ch6 L3696: Yor apologizes to Bond for the stew; Ch17 L9825: 'burning onions'; Ch17 L9829: 'potatoes are raw in the middle'
redundancy_findings.problem: Yor's bad cooking is independently proved in 6 chapters (Ch1, Ch5, Ch6, Ch10, Ch14, Ch17). Ch17's family dinner follows the same template as Ch6 (Yor cooks, food fails in a comic-specific way, family endures lovingly). By Ch17, the reader has absorbed this baseline. The gag has been independently proved 5 times before this chapter.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch17's dinner scene, let Yor's cooking simply exist without comic failure. The reader knows the baseline. Alternatively, invert the expectation: the food is imperfect but edible, marking growth rather than re-proving the gag.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch17's dinner scene does not re-prove Yor's cooking incompetence through the same comic template used in Ch1/Ch6.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch14 L7558: 'rice tasted like sesame and old metal'; Ch14 L7560: 'Yor apologized once for overcooking the vegetables'; established baseline in Ch1 L335, Ch6 L3696, Ch10 L5588
redundancy_findings.problem: Yor's bad cooking is re-proved in Ch14 with the same template (food described in comic-failure terms, Yor apologizes). This is the 5th independent proof of the same baseline. Ch14's cooking failure adds no new leverage or consequence.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut the cooking-failure details at L7558-7560. The planning dinner scene can reference food being served without re-proving the gag.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch14 does not independently re-prove Yor's cooking incompetence through comic food description.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch14 L7378: 'She did not tell them about telepathy...every useful thread she'd pulled had started with a thought she'd stolen'; established in Ch1 L17 (can't turn it off), Ch3 L850 (information nobody chose to give), Ch4 L2578 (its own rot), Ch16 L8878 (pivotal disclosure scene)
redundancy_findings.problem: Anya's 'can't tell anyone about telepathy' is enumerated at Ch14 L7378 as the 4th re-statement of this baseline. The reader has known since Ch1 that Anya keeps her telepathy secret. Ch16 L8878 is the pivotal disclosure that earns its enumeration. Ch14's re-statement is the most expendable because it re-proves what the reader already knows without adding new consequence.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: At L7378, cut the enumeration of who hasn't been told. Trust the reader to carry the secret-keeping premise from Ch1. If telepathy's relevance to the planning scene needs flagging, do it through action (Anya reading thoughts) rather than re-stating the concealment.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch14 does not re-enumerate that Anya has never told anyone about telepathy.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch3 quartet references at lines 1134, 1146, 1179, 1191, 1237, 1305, 1497, 1825, 1849 (9 instances); manuscript total: 26 instances across 5 chapters (Ch2:3, Ch3:9, Ch5:3, Ch8:4, Ch12:7)
redundancy_findings.problem: The word 'quartet' appears 26 times across 5 chapters. Ch3 accounts for 9 of these (35%). At 26 total, the target is keeping 4-6 instances. Ch3 can retain 2-3 (the initial arrival and one functional reference) and cut 6-7 instances.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce Ch3's quartet references from 9 to 2-3. Keep the Academy arrival reference (L1179) and one dialogue reference. Replace remaining instances with varied atmospheric detail or cut entirely where the quartet is background furniture.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch3 contains no more than 3 references to the quartet.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch12 quartet references at lines 6370, 6414, 6506, 6570, 6576, 6582, 6612, 6636 (7 instances in the Blackbell gala); manuscript total: 26 across 5 chapters
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch12 accounts for 7 of 26 total quartet references. The quartet becomes a running thread through the gala (Becky's frustration with it, mood-board joke), but 7 references in one chapter reinforces the reception-venue template redundancy. Target: keep 2-3 of the strongest.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Keep the mood-board joke (L6570-6576) and the final upbeat piece (L6636) as the two strongest. Cut quartet references at L6370, L6414, L6506, L6582, L6612 or replace with varied party atmosphere.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch12 contains no more than 3 references to the quartet.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch9 breath/breathe instances: L5261 (breathe past pain), L5267 (broke off to breathe), L5271 (breath catching), L5315 (breathing is a bad idea x2), L5393 (every breath), L5505 (hard breathing), L5517 (heartscaster breathing), L5535 (breathing too hard), L5537 (space of one breath), L5539 (breaking on breath), L5543 (enough to breathe), L5557 (breath of laughter), L5573 (still breathing); total 14 of 69 manuscript-wide
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch9 accounts for 14 of 69 manuscript-wide breath/breathe occurrences (20%). While the chapter's physical content (injuries, cold apartment, intimacy) makes breathing plot-relevant, the word has become the default physical-state cue. At 69 total across all 17 chapters, the target is 8-10 retained instances. Ch9 alone exceeds that target.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce Ch9's breath/breathe count from 14 to 5-6. Keep the strongest instances: 'breathing is a bad idea' joke (L5315), 'breaking on breath' (L5539), and 2-3 others. Replace remaining instances with varied physical cues (pulse, heat, cold, sound, movement).
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch9 contains no more than 6 instances of breath/breathe/breathing.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch4 fluorescent references at 4 locations; manuscript total: 18 across 9 chapters (Ch1:2, Ch3:2, Ch4:4, Ch6:1, Ch10:1, Ch11:3, Ch13:2, Ch15:1, Ch16:2)
redundancy_findings.problem: The word 'fluorescent' appears 18 times across 9 chapters. Ch4 is the densest with 4 instances. For 10-20 occurrences, the target is 4-6 retained instances manuscript-wide. Ch4 can retain 1-2 and cut 2-3 to bring the total below the keep-range ceiling.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce Ch4's fluorescent references from 4 to 1-2. Keep the strongest atmospheric instance. Replace others with varied institutional lighting descriptors (overhead, strip, white, buzzing).
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch4 contains no more than 2 references to fluorescent light.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch4 jaw-tightening: L2162 'Damian's jaw flexed once', L2276 'His jaw worked', L2490 'His jaw tightened'; also in Ch3 (L1409, L1827), Ch15 (L8203), Ch17 (L9697); total 7 Damian jaw-tightening instances across 4 chapters
redundancy_findings.problem: Damian's jaw-tightening/flexing/working is used 3 times within Ch4 alone and 7 times across 4 chapters (Ch3, Ch4, Ch15, Ch17). The gesture has become a default tension indicator for this character. Ch4's concentration of 3 instances in one chapter is the worst local cluster.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Keep one jaw-tightening in Ch4 (L2490 is the most dramatically weighted). Cut or replace L2162 and L2276 with varied tension indicators (hands, posture, silence, breath).
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch4 contains at most one jaw-tightening gesture for Damian.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch17 L9697: 'He stopped, jaw working'; earlier instances in Ch3 (L1409, L1827), Ch4 (L2162, L2276, L2490), Ch15 (L8203); 7 total Damian jaw-tightening instances
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch17 L9697 is the 7th instance of Damian's jaw-tightening across the manuscript and the 4th chapter using it. The gesture has been deployed with the same dramatic function (suppressed emotion) each time. By Ch17, the reader recognizes the tic.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: At L9697, replace the jaw gesture with a different physical tell or cut the gesture entirely, letting the interrupted speech carry the tension.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch17 does not use jaw-tightening/flexing/working for Damian.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch4 cheek-biting: L2210 'She bit the inside of her cheek hard enough to get copper', L2568 'She had bitten the inside of her cheek hard enough to split skin'; also Ch1 L185, Ch12 L6324, Ch17 L10049; total 5 instances across 4 chapters
redundancy_findings.problem: Anya biting the inside of her cheek appears 5 times across 4 chapters (Ch1, Ch4, Ch12, Ch17), with Ch4 containing two instances. The 'hard enough to [physical consequence]' construction appears in 3 of 5 instances. The gesture serves the same dramatic function each time (suppressing a visible reaction).
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut one of Ch4's two cheek-biting instances (L2568 is the most expendable since L2210 is closer to the moment of reaction). If the 'hard enough to' construction is kept anywhere, vary the physical consequence.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch4 contains at most one cheek-biting instance.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch17 L10049: 'Anya bit the inside of her cheek hard enough to keep her face steady'; earlier instances at Ch1 L185, Ch4 L2210, Ch4 L2568, Ch12 L6324; total 5 instances across 4 chapters
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch17 L10049 is the 5th instance of Anya biting the inside of her cheek and the 4th chapter using this gesture. The 'hard enough to' variant appears for the third time. By Ch17, the reader recognizes this as a default Anya tic rather than a fresh physical detail.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: At L10049, replace the cheek-biting with a different physical reaction to emotional pressure, or cut the gesture and let the dialogue carry the moment.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch17 does not use the cheek-biting gesture for Anya.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch16: 15 instances of 'something' in 5,108 prose words = 1 per 341 words; manuscript-wide: 141 instances in 102,891 words = 1 per 730 words (threshold: 1 per 800)
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch16 is the worst offender for 'something' density at 1 per 341 words — more than double the threshold rate. The hedge word 'something' appears 141 times manuscript-wide, exceeding the 1-per-800-words threshold. Ch16 contributes disproportionately to the overuse.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Audit Ch16 for every instance of 'something.' Replace with specific nouns, sensory details, or named emotions wherever the referent can be identified. Target reducing from 15 to 6 or fewer.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch16 contains no more than 6 instances of 'something' (rate of 1 per 850+ words).

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch17: 13 instances of 'something' in 5,924 prose words = 1 per 456 words; manuscript-wide: 141/102,891 = 1 per 730 words
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch17 has 13 instances of 'something' at a rate of 1 per 456 words, well above the 1/800 threshold. As the final chapter, hedge-word accumulation here undermines the novel's closing precision.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Audit Ch17 for 'something' and replace with specifics wherever the referent is identifiable. Target reducing from 13 to 7 or fewer.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch17 contains no more than 7 instances of 'something.'

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch4: 17 instances of 'something' in 7,786 prose words = 1 per 458 words; manuscript-wide: 141/102,891 = 1 per 730 words
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch4 has the highest absolute count (17 instances) of 'something' at a rate of 1 per 458 words. This is the novel's longest sustained Anya-Damian interaction chapter and the hedge word weakens the precision of their exchanges.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Audit Ch4 for 'something' and replace with specifics. Target reducing from 17 to 9 or fewer.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch4 contains no more than 9 instances of 'something.'

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch1: 10 instances of 'something' in 5,387 prose words = 1 per 539 words; manuscript-wide: 141/102,891 = 1 per 730 words
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch1 has 10 instances of 'something' at a rate of 1 per 539 words, above the 1/800 threshold. As the opening chapter, hedge-word density here sets a vague tonal precedent.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Audit Ch1 for 'something' and replace with specifics. Target reducing from 10 to 6 or fewer.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch1 contains no more than 6 instances of 'something.'

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch10: 10 instances of 'something' in 5,562 prose words = 1 per 556 words; manuscript-wide: 141/102,891 = 1 per 730 words
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch10 has 10 instances of 'something' at 1 per 556 words. This is Yor's assassination chapter where precision of observation is thematically important, making hedge words particularly counterproductive.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Audit Ch10 for 'something' and replace with operational or sensory specifics. Target reducing from 10 to 6 or fewer.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch10 contains no more than 6 instances of 'something.'

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch8: 13 instances of 'something' in 8,005 prose words = 1 per 616 words; manuscript-wide: 141/102,891 = 1 per 730 words
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch8 has 13 instances of 'something' at 1 per 616 words, above the 1/800 threshold. Combined with the chapter's other redundancy issues (telepathy re-statement, reception template), the hedge-word density compounds the impression of imprecision.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Audit Ch8 for 'something' and replace with specifics. Target reducing from 13 to 9 or fewer.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch8 contains no more than 9 instances of 'something.'

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch3: 11 instances of 'something' in 6,821 prose words = 1 per 620 words; manuscript-wide: 141/102,891 = 1 per 730 words
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch3 has 11 instances of 'something' at 1 per 620 words, above the 1/800 threshold.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Audit Ch3 for 'something' and replace with specifics. Target reducing from 11 to 8 or fewer.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch3 contains no more than 8 instances of 'something.'

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch13: 9 instances of 'something' in 5,705 prose words = 1 per 634 words; manuscript-wide: 141/102,891 = 1 per 730 words
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch13 has 9 instances of 'something' at 1 per 634 words, above the 1/800 threshold.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Audit Ch13 for 'something' and replace with specifics. Target reducing from 9 to 7 or fewer.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch13 contains no more than 7 instances of 'something.'

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch15 L8591: 'she saw four men in civilian clothes moving with military economy'; Ch15 L8645: Yuri's radio report 'Four hostiles down'; Ch17 L10229: 'a woman who could kill three men in under a minute'
consistency_findings.problem: Ch15 shows Yor confronting and neutralizing four hostile operatives on the South Terrace (L8591), confirmed by Yuri's post-action report of 'Four hostiles down' (L8645). Ch17 L10229 summarizes this as 'kill three men,' contradicting the established count.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Change 'three men' to 'four men' at Ch17 L10229 to match the Ch15 action and Yuri's confirmed count.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: Ch17's reference to Yor's terrace action specifies four, not three.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch7 L4295-4305: box contains 'report card, spelling certificate second place, ribbon from athletics day, festival program, dry pen, photograph'; Ch13 L7179: box contains 'school blazer outgrown at thirteen, note from mother, runner-up certificate from a math competition, three pens'
consistency_findings.problem: Damian's memorabilia box is inventoried in Ch7 with specific items (spelling certificate, one dry pen, athletics ribbon, festival program). Ch13 re-inventories the same box with substantially different items (math certificate, three pens, school blazer, mother's note). The certificates differ (spelling vs. math), the pen count differs (one vs. three), and several items appear in one inventory but not the other.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Reconcile Ch13's box inventory with Ch7's established contents. The reader who remembers the Ch7 inventory will notice the discrepancy. Either match the items exactly or have Damian notice items he missed before rather than presenting a contradictory complete inventory.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: Ch13's box contents are consistent with Ch7's established inventory — same certificates, same pen count, with any new items framed as previously unmentioned rather than replacing prior items.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch7 L4299: 'Damian found himself in the third row, second from the right'; Ch16 L9048: 'Damian was in the front row already looking annoyed'; Ch16 L8856: 'Old school shoes lay on top' (not in Ch7 or Ch13 inventories)
consistency_findings.problem: The class photo places Damian in the 'third row, second from the right' in Ch7 L4299 but in the 'front row' in Ch16 L9048. While these could theoretically be different photos from different years (age 10 in Ch7, age 6 in Ch16), the box is presented as containing one photo, and the reader would expect consistency. Additionally, Ch16 introduces 'old school shoes' on top of the box that appear in neither prior inventory.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Match Damian's photo position to Ch7's established 'third row, second from the right,' or explicitly establish that the box contains multiple photos from different years. Reconcile the school shoes with prior inventories.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: Damian's row position in the class photo is consistent across all chapters, and any new box items are reconciled with prior inventories.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch17 L10191: '*Three months ago. I knew her name. I didn't know the rest of her yet.*'; Ch12 gala is late October; Ch17 is late March (L9613: 'late-March brightness')
consistency_findings.problem: Damian's thought 'Three months ago. I knew her name' at Ch17 L10191 is triggered by the word 'birthday.' If referencing the gala where he learned her name (late October), the interval to late March is ~5 months, not 3. The line is defensible if 'three months ago' refers to the birthday party (Anya's 21st, plausibly in January), by which time he already knew her name — but the ambiguity risks confusing attentive readers.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Clarify the temporal anchor. If referring to the gala, change 'three months' to 'five months.' If referring to the birthday party, add a word to make the referent unambiguous (e.g., 'At the party three months ago').
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: The temporal reference at L10191 unambiguously identifies its referent event and the elapsed time is arithmetically correct.
